attributes.stem: A six-month-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of runny nose and dry cough, progressing to wheeze and difficulty breathing this morning. He has not been feeding well. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears unwell and irritable. His pulse rate is 150/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 40/min, SpO2 89% on room air, and temperature 37.1°C. Respiratory examination reveals nasal flaring, subcostal recession, and a tracheal tug. Auscultation of the chest demonstrates diffuse wheeze.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with classic signs and symptoms of bronchiolitis, a common viral lower respiratory tract infection in young children. The diagnosis is based on clinical features, including an initial upper respiratory tract infection followed by respiratory distress, tachypnoea, and auscultatory findings of wheeze or crackles.

Oxygen therapy is the most appropriate first step in management for this patient. His oxygen saturation of 89% is below the generally accepted threshold of 90% for initiating oxygen therapy in bronchiolitis. Correcting hypoxemia is crucial in managing respiratory distress and preventing further complications.

Oral antibiotics are not routinely indicated in bronchiolitis, which is typically caused by viruses. Antibiotics should only be considered if there is strong suspicion of a secondary bacterial infection or pneumonia, which is not evident in this case.

Oral prednisolone is not recommended in the standard treatment of bronchiolitis. While there are ongoing studies investigating the role of steroids in bronchiolitis, current evidence does not support their routine use.

Supplemental fluids are important in the management of bronchiolitis, especially when feeding is compromised. This patient will likely require fluid support given his poor feeding. However, addressing the hypoxia with oxygen therapy takes precedence as the first step in management.

Nebulised salbutamol is not indicated in infants under 12 months old with bronchiolitis. Studies have shown that bronchodilators are not effective in this age group for this condition.

After initiating oxygen therapy, the next steps in management would include assessing the need for supplemental fluids (oral, nasogastric, or intravenous depending on the severity), monitoring respiratory status, and providing supportive care. It's important to note that bronchiolitis is generally self-limiting, and management focuses on supportive measures rather than specific treatments.
learningPoints: Bronchiolitis is common in infants and does not usually require imaging or antibiotics.
Oxygen therapy is the mainstay treatment of bronchiolitis in those with hypoxia.
Salbutamol is not indicated in patients less than 12 months old with bronchiolitis.
references: Yusuf F, Prayle AP, Yanney MP. β2-agonists do not work in children under 2 years of age: myth or maxim? Breathe (Sheff). 2019;15(4):273-276. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6885336/#:~:text=Infants%20do%20have%20functioning%20β,one%20of%20life%20%5B13%5D.

South Australian Paediatric Clinical Practice Guidelines: Bronchiolitis in Children [online]. 2018. Available at: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/0a3fd50040d03f4d96fbbe40b897efc8/Bronchiolitis_in_Children_Paed_v2_0.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-0a3fd50040d03f4d96fbbe40b897efc8-oAxjYF9
Bronchiolitis. In: Royal Children's Hospital Clinical Practice Guidelines [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Bronchiolitis/
